summarization.a: Conceptual model of a spatial, rotary ASALD reactor showing one inlet/outlet configuration and reagent positioning, illustrating gas distribution over three wafers on a rotating plate.
summarization.b: Conceptual model of a spatial, rotary ASALD reactor showing a second inlet/outlet configuration and reagent positioning, illustrating gas distribution over three wafers on a rotating plate.
summarization.c: Conceptual model of a spatial, rotary ASALD reactor showing a third inlet/outlet configuration and reagent positioning, illustrating gas distribution over three wafers on a rotating plate.
summarization.d: Conceptual model of a spatial, rotary ASALD reactor showing a fourth inlet/outlet configuration and reagent positioning, illustrating gas distribution over three wafers on a rotating plate.

d.question: List the aspects of reactor performance that can be evaluated by comparing the four spatial, rotary ASALD reactor designs.
d.answer: Gas flow distribution, Reactant exposure uniformity, Effect of inlet and outlet geometry, Suitability for multiscale and CFD simulations

b.question: Do asymmetric inlet geometries influence gas flow behavior differently compared to symmetric inlet designs?
b.answer: Yes. Asymmetric inlet geometries alter the flow distribution and interaction of reactant gases compared to symmetric designs, which can significantly affect uniformity and reactor performance.

a.question: How do the different spatial, rotary ASALD reactor configurations operate to expose wafers to multiple reaction zones?
a.answer: In all four reactor configurations, three wafers are mounted on a rotating plate at the bottom of the reactor. As the plate rotates, the wafers sequentially pass through spatially separated reaction zones defined by different inlet and outlet geometries. Reagents and inert gases are introduced through specifically shaped inlets, while outlets remove gases, allowing controlled exposure of the wafer surfaces without temporal pulsing.

c.question: What structural features vary among the four reactor models shown in Figure 5?
c.answer: The inlet and outlet geometries vary in shape (ring or annular sector) and positioning (symmetric or asymmetric) across the four reactor models.
